description: This figure presents a density-composition plot showing the variation of alloy density (g/cm³) as a function of aluminum content (wt.-% Al) for lightweight steel design. Open triangles represent experimental density data for binary Fe-Al alloys compiled by Frommeyer and Drewes (2000), while open squares denote the quaternary 0.2C-8.5Mn (wt.%) alloys studied in the present work, measured by picnometry. A solid continuous line shows the calculated density for the quaternary system obtained via Thermo-Calc using the Lindahl and Selleby database assessment. The data reveal a clear linear decreasing trend in density with increasing aluminum addition, dropping from approximately 7.3 g/cm³ at 5–6 wt.% Al to about 6.55 g/cm³ at 13 wt.% Al. The experimental points for the quaternary alloys lie slightly below the binary Fe-Al dataset, suggesting a minor additional density reduction effect from Mn additions. This figure establishes density reduction as a critical design parameter for automotive lightweighting steels and validates that Thermo-Calc calculations reproduce the experimental density trend for the quaternary Fe-Mn-Al-C system.

Figure 2 displays isoplethal sections of a quaternary 8Mn-0.2C (wt.%) Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy calculated as a function of 0–10 wt.-% Al and 600–1400 °C using three different CALPHAD databases. Subfigure (a) shows the Hallstedt database with the Chin et al. assessment (PrecHiMn-01), (b) shows the Umino et al. assessment (PrecHiMn-02), and (c) shows the Lindahl and Selleby assessment (PrecHiMn-03). All three panels predict a large FCC single-phase loop at low Al contents, a Liquid + BCC field at high temperature and high Al, and an intermediate BCC + FCC two-phase region. Major discrepancies appear below approximately 900 °C. Panel (a) predicts complex low-temperature equilibria involving CEMENTITE, M5C2, and κ-phase coexisting with BCC and FCC. Panel (b) simplifies the low-temperature region to BCC + FCC + κ, omitting all carbide phases. Panel (c) captures a BCC + FCC + κ three-phase domain and a narrow BCC + CEMENTITE + FCC field near the Fe-rich corner, aligning with the caption and citation statements that this latest assessment best reproduces the Fe-rich corner and the κ-phase (j-phase) stability. These differences highlight that the choice of thermodynamic database critically influences predicted austenite decomposition and κ-phase precipitation in low-density steels.

Figure 3 presents CALPHAD-calculated austenite (FCC) phase fraction as a function of temperature for an 8Mn-0.2C (wt.%) low-density steel alloy containing either 6 wt.% Al or 8 wt.% Al, using three thermodynamic databases: PrecHiMn-01 (light gray solid), PrecHiMn-02 (black dashed), and PrecHiMn-03 (gray solid). For each alloy composition, the curves rise from near-zero fraction at approximately 600 °C to a maximum between roughly 900 and 1000 °C before declining toward zero near 1400 °C. The 8 wt.% Al variant consistently exhibits higher predicted austenite stability than the 6 wt.% Al variant across all databases. Notably, the PrecHiMn-02 database predicts substantially higher peak austenite fractions, reaching about 0.75 for 8% Al, compared with PrecHiMn-01 and PrecHiMn-03 which peak around 0.43–0.48. The first and third database versions yield similar curve shapes but are shifted in temperature. The caption and citation note persistent discrepancies among databases regarding the fcc + bcc + κ three-phase domain and emphasize that experimental validation is still needed, underscoring that accurate thermodynamic descriptions are critical for alloy composition and continuous annealing process design in these lightweight steels.

The figure displays an isothermal section at 850 °C for the iron-rich corner of the Fe-Mn-Al-C system with a fixed 8 wt.% Al content, mapping phase stability as a function of Mn (0–25 wt.%) and C (0–1.0 wt.%). The diagram identifies phase fields including single-phase bcc (ferrite), single-phase fcc (austenite), two-phase bcc + fcc, and κ-phase-bearing regions such as bcc + κ, bcc + fcc + κ, and fcc + κ. At low Mn and low C, bcc is stable with minor carbon solubility. Increasing C promotes κ-phase formation, whereas higher Mn is required to suppress κ and stabilize fcc or the bcc + fcc dual-phase field. This phase diagram supports the compositional design of three low-density steel families—ferritic, austenite-ferrite dual-phase, and austenitic/TRIPLEX—and highlights the Mn and C thresholds needed to avoid unwanted κ-phase precipitation during heat treatments like continuous annealing.

Figure 5 displays typical microstructures of three families of high-Al low-density steels for automotive lightweighting. Panel (a) shows an equiaxed fully ferritic grain structure (scale bar 40 μm). Panel (b) is an EBSD phase map (scale bar 50 μm) of a two-phase austenite-ferrite alloy, with austenite colored in red and arranged in elongated bands within the ferrite matrix. Panel (c) is an EBSD image (scale bar 20 μm) of a finer austenite-based TRIPLEX microstructure, where small ferrite regions are marked in blue; the caption notes that the nanometric j-phase precipitates are not resolved due to their size. Together, the images demonstrate how increasing Mn and C additions stabilize austenite, evolving the microstructure from single-phase ferritic Fe-Al alloys to duplex and then austenite-rich grades containing minor ferrite and intragranular nanometric precipitates. This supports the central thesis that compositional design in high-Al steels produces tailored multiphase microstructures enabling density reduction alongside mechanical performance.

This figure presents a strain rate–temperature map, caption states for a ferritic Fe-8 wt.% Al steel, displaying the activation domain of discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) based on hot torsion tests. The horizontal axis shows temperature from 850 to 1150 °C, while the vertical axis gives strain rate on a logarithmic scale from 0.01 to 100 s⁻¹. Green circles mark conditions where DDRX is not activated, whereas red circles indicate DDRX-activated conditions, separated by a dashed boundary line. Experimental data cluster at 900, 1000, and 1100 °C over strain rates of approximately 0.1 to 10 s⁻¹. The map shows that DDRX is promoted at higher temperatures and higher strain rates, with the critical strain rate for activation shifting downward as temperature increases. This processing map guides microstructure control during hot deformation, distinguishing the DDRX domain from the continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) regime reported in the accompanying text.

This figure presents κ-phase microstructures in a 2Mn-8Al-0.2C (wt.%) low-density steel under different thermal histories. Panel (a) is an optical micrograph showing a nonlamellar eutectoid-like microstructure after cooling from 1400°C at 10°C/s and quenching at 870°C, where κ-phase and ferrite (α) precipitate at austenite (γ)/ferrite interfaces. Panels (b) and (c) are scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs after deep etching, revealing three-dimensional morphologies. Figure 7b shows κ-phase growing along the long axis of austenite with complex blade-like precipitation. Figure 7c shows the microstructure after 15 min at 870°C, where isothermal treatment in the ferrite+κ domain promotes complete decomposition of austenite, leaving a cocoon-like κ-phase structure encasing the former austenite region. The comparison demonstrates the evolution from interfacial precipitation during continuous cooling to fully decomposed structures during isothermal holding.

Figure 8 presents TEM characterization of microstructural evolution in a 28.8Mn–9.4Al–0.92C (wt.%) steel during aging at 500°C. Each panel pairs a selected-area electron diffraction pattern labeled [100]γ—caption states the [001] zone axis—with a dark-field image taken using {100} superlattice reflections. Panel (a) shows the initial state after 2 min, where diffuse superlattice spots indicate early-stage ordering but precipitates are difficult to image. After 16 min (b), reflections become sharper and faint precipitate contrast appears. In (c), after 4 h, distinct round nanoprecipitates are visible; the text reports their size as smaller than 5 nm. In (d), after 64 h, the precipitates exhibit a cuboidal morphology reported as 5–10 nm and are aligned along the ⟨100⟩ directions of the austenite matrix under the g = 100 imaging condition. Together, these images document the nucleation, growth, and morphological transition of ordered κ-phase precipitates, revealing a progressive coarsening mechanism in this austenitic low-density steel.

This figure presents TEM characterization of ferrite ordering evolution in a 29Mn-9.6Al-0.56C (wt.%) low-density steel during aging at 550°C. Subfigure (a) shows the as-quenched condition: a selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern along the [011]α zone axis displays extra superlattice reflections, where {111} spots indicate the DO₃ structure and {200} spots correspond to either DO₃ or B2 ordering; the intensity difference between these reflections reveals a mixture of B2- and DO₃-ordered structures, while the {200} dark-field image shows nanoscale ordered domains. Subfigure (b) presents the sample after 1 h at 550°C: the SAED pattern shows equal intensities for {111} and {200} reflections, indicating a fully ordered DO₃ ferrite, and the {200} dark-field image reveals coarsened ordered domains. The accompanying text reports the material was solution-treated at 850°C for 10 min and water-quenched before aging. These observations confirm that aluminum enrichment in ferrite drives a transition to full DO₃ order during aging.

The figure is a line graph showing tensile strength (TS, MPa) versus annealing temperature (T, °C) for a series of 8.5Mn-0.2C (wt.%) austenite-ferrite alloys. Data series in the legend correspond to three compositions—6Al, 6Al+XSi, and 7Al—each tested at three hold times (1 min, 136 sec, and 5 min), represented by green diamonds, red squares, and blue triangles, respectively. For every composition and time, TS decreases monotonically as annealing temperature rises from 800°C to 1000°C, reflecting softening during recrystallization. The 6Al+XSi alloy consistently records the highest strength (approximately 800 MPa at 800°C), followed by 7Al (~758 MPa) and base 6Al (~743 MPa), evidencing solid-solution strengthening from added Si and higher Al. Prolonged annealing generally causes minor additional strength loss. Collectively, the data establish that while Al and Si substantially raise the strength of two-phase low-density steels, this advantage is progressively eroded at higher annealing temperatures.

Figure 11 presents EBSD microstructural characterization of an 8.5Mn-6Al-XSi-0.2C (wt.%) low-density steel annealed for 136 s at two temperatures. Subfigures (a) and (c) correspond to annealing at 800°C, while (b) and (d) correspond to 1000°C. Panels (a) and (b) are phase maps in which austenite is highlighted in red on a band contrast (BC) background, showing that ferrite recrystallization is incomplete at 800°C with pronounced pancake-shaped grains, whereas at 1000°C the ferrite is more fully recrystallized and equiaxed. Panels (c) and (d) are inverse pole figure (IPF) maps of ferrite, revealing that the 800°C condition retains a highly elongated, layered grain structure with greater orientation variation, while the 1000°C condition exhibits larger, more equiaxed ferrite grains. The comparison demonstrates that annealing temperature strongly governs recrystallization kinetics and ferrite grain morphology in this two-phase lightweight steel.

Figure 12 displays engineering stress–strain curves for a fully austenitic 0.92C-28.8Mn-9.4Al (wt.%) alloy tested at six austenitic grain sizes: 1.65 μm, 2.2 μm, 8.4 μm, 12.4 μm, 21 μm, and 40 μm, distinguished by solid, dashed, and dotted line styles. The curves demonstrate that decreasing grain size markedly raises both yield and ultimate tensile strengths—the finest 1.65 μm material reaches approximately 980 MPa and fractures near 48% elongation—whereas the coarsest 40 μm specimen exhibits lower strength (~800 MPa) but superior ductility extending beyond 60% elongation. Uniform and total elongation increase with increasing grain size, yet the accompanying text notes a saturation effect above approximately 10 μm. The caption and associated discussion report a classical Hall–Petch relation with coefficients of 461 MPa·μm^1/2 for yield strength and 351 MPa·μm^1/2 for tensile strength. Collectively, this figure evidences the strength–ductility trade-off governed by grain-boundary strengthening in this low-density austenitic steel and identifies a grain-size regime where further coarsening no longer benefits formability.

The figure shows a transparent wireframe rendering of an automotive body-in-white with two front longitudinal structural members highlighted in blue, schematically indicating the primary crash-absorbing components assessed in the study. According to the extracted text, these correspond to the front side members evaluated alongside A-pillars in LS-DYNA finite element simulations of a EuroNCAP 40% overlap frontal impact, benchmarking low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steels against a conventional DP600 dual-phase steel. The evaluated alloys include an austenite-ferrite grade (7.4Al-8.2Mn-0.2C wt.%) and a fully austenitic grade (9.3Al-25.8Mn-0.96C wt.%). The text reports that the austenite-ferrite alloy achieves approximately 21% mass savings in the front side member (from 5.88 kg to 4.63 kg) and A-pillar relative to DP600, while the fully austenitic alloy attains about 32–33% weight reduction (front side member to 3.99 kg; A-pillar to 4.12 kg) with adapted thicknesses to meet energy absorption and section force criteria. The figure thus visually anchors the computational lightweighting claims to specific crash-critical front-end structures.

The figure presents a three-dimensional wireframe or semi-transparent schematic of a passenger-car body-in-white, with two front structural components highlighted in red. These highlighted regions correspond to critical energy-absorption or anti-intrusion members—likely the front side members and/or A-pillars—fabricated from low-density steels investigated in this work. The diagram illustrates the targeted automotive application positions for lightweight high-aluminum steels, specifically an austenite-ferrite grade (7.4Al–8.2Mn–0.2C wt.%) and a fully austenitic grade (9.3Al–25.8Mn–0.96C wt.%), benchmarked against conventional DP600 dual-phase steel. According to the paper’s finite-element crash simulations (LS-DYNA, EuroNCAP 40 % overlap front crash), substitution of these low-density alloys enables mass reductions of approximately 21 % for the austenite-ferrite concept and 32–33 % for the austenitic concept in these specific components while maintaining energy-absorption and section-force performance. The visualization thus serves to contextualize the macroscale structural implementation and weight-saving potential of advanced Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels in automotive body structures.
